coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical biosensor based on Au5Ir@graphene quantum dot nanocomposite and DNA walker for detection of atrazine in environmental water with extremely high sensitivity and selectivity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Pan
Ruiyi, Li
Xie, Wang
Yirui, Shen
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Existing electrochemical sensors are inadequate for detecting pesticides at extremely low level. This study presents an electrochemical biosensor based on gold-iridium alloy (Au5Ir) and DNA walker for detection of atrazine. Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD nanocomposite was synthesized via the reduction of chloroauric acid and iridium trichloride using arginine and folic acid-functionalized boron and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dot (RFBP-GQD). The synergy between Au and Ir, along with the formation of Schottky heterojunction, enhances the catalytic activity. The Au5Ir was covalently conjugated with hairpin DNA and thionine forming a redox probe that was used to construct the electrochemical biosensor for atrazine detection, coupled with a DNA walker mechanism. Atrazine triggers the DNA walker, leading to the introduction of multiple redox probes onto the gold electrode surface. The oxidation and reduction of thionine molecules within these redox probes elicit highly sensitive electrochemical response. The integration of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD catalysis with the DNA walker results in significant signal amplification. The biosensor exhibits superior sensitivity and selectivity compared to other atrazine sensors, with a linear detection range from 1×10−18 to 1×10−12 M and a low detection limit of 3.4×10−19 M (S/N=3). The proposed analytical method was successfully used for detection of atrazine in environmental water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical biosensor
Atrazine detection
Gold-iridium alloy
Graphene quantum dot
Schottky heterojunction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (A), TEM (B) and HRTEM images (C) and element mappinngs of C (D), N (E), B (F), P (G), Ir (H) and Au (I) of the as-synthesized Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD.
XRD pattern (A), FTIR spectrum (B) and XPS spectra of Ir4f (C), Au4f (D), P2p (E), B1s (F), C1s (G), N1s (H) and O1s (I) of the as-synthesized Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD.

Biosensor construction (A), CV curves at scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (B) and Nyquist plots (Insert: equivalent circuit) (C) in 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in the PBS of pH 7.4 of bare GE (a), H4/GE (b), MCH/H4/GE (C), MCH/H4/GE incubated at 37 °C for 60 min in a tripedal DNA walker solution in the absence (d) and the presence (e) of H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI, electrophoregram (D) of AP (lane 1), AS (lane 2), H1 (lane 3), H2 (lane 4), H3 (lane 5), H4 (lane 6), H5 (lane 7), AP+AS (lane 8), AS+H1 (lane 9), AS+H1+H2 (lane 10), unannealedH4+H5 (lane 11), AP+AS+H1+H2+H3 (lane 12), AP+AS+H1+H2+H4+H4+H5 (lane 13), and AP/AS+H1 (lane 14), CV curves of MCH/H4/GE in 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in the PBS of pH 7.4 at scan rate of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60,70, 80, 90 and 100 mV s−1 (E), plots (F) of CV peak current vs. scan rate, and CV curves for 1st and 100th measurements (G).

CV curves at the scan rate of 100 mV s−1 (A) and DPV curves (B) in the PBS of pH 7.4 in the absence and the presence of 1×10−12 M atrazine, plot of the DPV peak current vs. the incubation time in the presence of 1×10−12 M atrazine (C), plot of the DPV peak current vs. the H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI volume in the presence of 1×10−12 M atrazine (D), DPV curves in the presence of 1×10−18, 1×10−17, 5×10−17, 1×10−16, 5×10−16, 1×10−15, 5×10−15, 1×10−14, 5×10−14, 1×10−13, 5×10−13 and 1×10−12 M atrazine (from bottom to top) (E), relationship curve of the DPV peak current with the logarithmic value of atrazine concentration (F), and DPV peak currents in the presence of 1×10−12 M of atrazine for different biosensors (G), DPV peak currents in the presence 1×10−12 M of atrazine for different storage times (H), and DPV peak currents in the presence of 1×10−12 M fipronil, cypermethrin, acetamiprid, thiabendazole, chlorpyrifos, or atrazine (I).
Comparison of analytical performance of electrochemical method for detection of atrazine.

row.entry: Paddy water, paddy soil and polished rice
Paddy water, paddy soil and polished rice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for detection of atrazine in environmental water samples by proposed method and HPLC-MS.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical biosensor based on Au5Ir@graphene quantum dot nanocomposite and DNA walker for detection of atrazine in environmental water with extremely high sensitivity and selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Existing electrochemical sensors are inadequate for detecting pesticides at extremely low level. This study presents an electrochemical biosensor based on gold-iridium alloy (Au5Ir) and DNA walker for detection of atrazine. Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD nanocomposite was synthesized via the reduction of chloroauric acid and iridium trichloride using arginine and folic acid-functionalized boron and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dot (RFBP-GQD). The synergy between Au and Ir, along with the formation of Schottky heterojunction, enhances the catalytic activity. The Au5Ir was covalently conjugated with hairpin DNA and thionine forming a redox probe that was used to construct the electrochemical biosensor for atrazine detection, coupled with a DNA walker mechanism. Atrazine triggers the DNA walker, leading to the introduction of multiple redox probes onto the gold electrode surface. The oxidation and reduction of thionine molecules within these redox probes elicit highly sensitive electrochemical response. The integration of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD catalysis with the DNA walker results in significant signal amplification. The biosensor exhibits superior sensitivity and selectivity compared to other atrazine sensors, with a linear detection range from 1×10−18 to 1×10−12 M and a low detection limit of 3.4×10−19 M (S/N=3). The proposed analytical method was successfully used for detection of atrazine in environmental water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Atrazine has been widely used in agricultural production for weed control because of high efficacy and cost-effectiveness [1]. However, atrazine can enter human body through water, dermal exposure, and food chain, raising significant health concerns [2]. By inhibiting the expression of Srd5α1 gene, atrazine acts as a potent endocrine disruptor [3]. The prolonged exposure to atrazine has been associated with weight loss, heart congestion, cardiovascular issue, and an increased risk of cancer [4]. Therefore, developing a reliable method for detecting atrazine in environmental water at extremely low level is crucial for safeguarding human health.

Several analytical methods are employed for detection of atrazine, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], gas chromatography (GC) [6], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [7], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [8], fluorescence method [9], and electrochemical sensor [10]. HPLC can offer high sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and the ability to detect multi component simultaneously, though it requires expensive instrumentation. GC requires the atrazine derivatization, as it is difficult to vaporize. Techniques such as CE, SERS, and fluorescence method exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity; however, their performance can be influenced by factors such as temperature and ionic strength. Electrochemical sensor has garnered significant interest for atrazine detection due to its high sensitivity, selectivity, rapid response time, and ease of miniaturization [11].

The materials used in electrochemical sensors for detection of atrazine mainly include Co3O4 [12], Ni-Fe layered double oxide [13], MnO2-NiO composites [14], Fe3O4 [15], Al2NiCoO5 [16], graphene oxide [17], carbon black [18], ZnO [19], and silver nanoparticle [20]. While materials such as Co3O4, MnO2-NiO, Al2NiCoO5, Fe3O4, and ZnO exhibit good catalytic properties, they often suffer from poor electrical conductivity. Graphene oxide and carbon black provide high electrical conductivity, but their limited chemical stability may lead to many side reactions. Due to their superior electrical conductivity, catalytic activity, and chemical inertness, noble metals have become the preferred materials for constructing the electrochemical biosensors. However, facile synthesis and performance optimization of noble metal nanomaterials for electrochemical biosensors remain a challenge.

In the study, we reported the synthesis of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD nanocomposite by reducing chloroauric acid and iridium trichloride with arginine and folic acid-functionalized boron and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dot (RFBP-GQD). The synergy between Au and Ir, along with the formation of Schottky heterojunction, significantly improves the catalytic activity. The electrochemical biosensor incorporating Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD and a DNA walker exhibits exceptionally high sensitivity and selectivity, and was successfully employed for atrazine detection in environmental water samples.

Aptamer (AP, TGTACCGTCTGAGCGATTCGTACTTTATTCGGGAAGGGTATCAGCGGGG TTCAACAAGCCAGTCAGTGTTAAGGAGTGC), auxiliary strand DNA (AS, TTG TTGAACCC CGCTGATACCC), hairpin DNA 1 (H1, 5′-ACTTTGGGTATCAGCGGGGTTCAACAAAAAG TAGATCGTTTGTTGAACCCCGCTCGACATCTAACCTAGCTCACTGA-3′), hairpin DNA 2 (H2, 5′-AGCGGGGTTCAACAAACGATCTACTTTCCGCTGATACCCAAAGTAGAT CGTTTG TCGACATCTAACCTAGCTCACTGA-3′), hairpin DNA 3 (H3, 5′-ACAAACGATCTACTTTG GGTATCAGCGGTTGTTGAACCCCGCTGATACCCAAAGTCGACATCTAACCTAGCTCACTGA-3′), hairpin DNA 4 (H4, 5′-SH-GTCAGTGAGCTAGGTTAGATGTCGCCATGTGTAGACG ACATCTAACCTAGC-3′), and hairpin DNA 5 (H5, 5′-AGATGTCGTCTACACATGG CGACAT CTAACCTAGCCCATGTGTAGA-SH-3′) were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). To form the hairpin structure, H1, H2, H3, H4 and H5 were rapidly heated at 95 °C for 5 min and subsequently cooled to room temperature.

Typically, 1.27 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 solution was combined with 0.3175 mL of 10 mM IrCl3xH2O solution and subsequently heated at 95 °C for 5 min. Following this, 10 mL of 5 mg mL−1 RFBP-GQD solution was added, and the mixture was heated at 100 °C for 10 min, cooled to room temperature, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD was washed with ultrapure water and then re-dispersed in 1 mL of ultrapure water.

H5 (100 µL, 10 mM) was combined with an equal volume of 1 mM TCEP solution and allowed to incubate for 60 min. Subsequently, 300 µL of the Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD stock solution was added, and the mixture was incubated for an additional 24 h. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed with ultrapure water. The resulting H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD was resuspended in 100 µL of 0.1 mM thionine (THI) solution. Following a 12 h incubation period, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed with ultrapure water. The final H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI product was dispersed in 500 µL of TE solution.

AP solution (10 µM) was combined with an equal volume of AS solution (10 µM) and subsequently incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Thereafter, 5 µL of this mixture was merged with 20 µL of an atrazine standard solution of varying concentrations, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, 10 µL of the resultant solution was mixed with 5 µL each of 10 µM H1, 10 µM H2, and 10 µM H3, and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h to yield a CHA solution.

H4 (10 µL, 10 µM) was combined with 20 µL of 1 mM TCEP solution and allowed to incubate for 30 min. Subsequently, 5 μL of this mixture was deposited onto the surface of a gold electrode (GE, with diameter of 2 mm), incubated for 1.5 h, rinsed with ultrapure water, and dried using a stream of N2 to create H4/GE. Next, 5 µL of 1 mM MCH solution was applied to the surface of H4/GE. Following an incubation period of 2 h, the surface was washed with ultrapure water and dried once more with N2.

The prepared CHA solution of 20 µL was combined with 10 µL of the H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI solution and 20 µL of TE buffer. Subsequently, 5 µL of this mixture was deposited onto the surface of prepared biosensor. After incubated at 37 ℃ for 60 min, it was washed three times with the PBS at pH 7.4 and dried with a stream of N2. The resulting biosensor was subjected to differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurement in the PBS of pH 7.4.

Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD nanocomposite was designed and synthesized by the reduction of HAuCl4 and IrCl3 using RFBP-GQD. Due to strong reducing capacity, RFBP-GQD reduces Au3+ and Ir3+ to Au+ and Ir2+, respectively, finally leading to the formation of Au5Ir nanoalloy. In addition, RFBP-GQD binds to the Au5Ir nanoalloy, forming a specialized modification layer, which significantly enhances the structural stability and affinity of the Au5Ir nanoalloy with polar electrolytes due to its strong coordination ability. Furthermore, the unique semiconductor properties of RFBP-GQD facilitate the formation of a Schottky heterojunction with Au₅Ir nanoalloy, which accelerates the electron transfer from the Au₅Ir to the graphene. The synthesis experiment demonstrates that the yellow-green mixed solution of HAuCl4 and IrCl3 rapidly fades within 2 min (Fig. s1), as the yellow Au3+ and green Ir3+ are reduced to colorless Au+ and Ir2+. Over time, the color deepens and turns black, and after 5 min, precipitation begins to form, indicating the formation of the Au5Ir nanoalloy. The precipitation reaches its maximum after approximately 8 min, confirming the nearly complete formation of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD. These results validate the successful synthesis of the Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD nanocomposite.

Fig. 1 presents the scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, and element mappings of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD. The Au5Ir nanoalloy exhibits irregular spherical morphology with an average particle size of 52.6 ± 0.78 nm. The SEM image reveals the presence of numerous thin sheets, indicative of graphene. The HRTEM image displays a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of the Au5Ir nanoalloy. A coating layer is visible on the surface of Au5Ir nanoalloy, confirming the successful modification of the nanoalloy by RFBP-GQD. Elemental mapping analysis indicates the spatial distribution of carbon, nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, iridium, and gold. The carbon mapping appears bright and dense due to the high content of RFBP-GQD, while nitrogen, boron, and phosphorus show darker, sparser distributions due to their lower concentrations. The element mappings of Au and Ir align well with the morphology of Au5Ir nanoalloy. The molar ratio of Au and Ir, determined by ICP-MS, is approximately 5:1.

Fig. 2 illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD. The XRD pattern displays four distinct diffraction peaks at 38.76 °, 44.47 °, 65.01 °, and 77.75 °, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of the Au5Ir nanoalloy. Due to the higher Au content, the XRD pattern closely resembles that of Au nanoparticle (JCPDS no. 04–0784). The FTIR spectrum shows seven distinct absorption bands. The broad band around 3430 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, N-H, and =C-H bonds. The peak at 3144 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of =C-H bond, while the peak at 1695 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CO bond. The peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 correspond to CC and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively, and the peak at 1007 cm−1 is attributed to C-O out-of-plane stretching. The XPS spectrum of Ir4f displays four peaks at 61.40, 62.05, 64.22, and 64.89 eV. The two peaks at 61.40 and 64.22 eV correspond to the Ir4f7/2 and Ir4f5/2 of Ir2+, indicating the existence of Ir2+specie. The peaks at 62.05 and 64.89 eV correspond to the Ir4f7/2 and Ir4f5/2 of Ir0, confirming the presence of Ir0. The peak area analysis suggests that Ir0 is the predominant species. Similarly, the XPS spectrum of Au4f shows four distinct peaks at 86.74, 85.08, 87.68, and 89.26 eV. The peaks at 86.74 and 87.68 eV correspond to the Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 of Au0, while the peaks at 85.08 and 89.26 eV correspond to the Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 of Au+, signifying the presence of Au+. The peak area corresponding to Au0 is larger than that of Au+, confirming the presence of both Au0 and Au+, with Au⁰ being the dominant species. The XPS spectrum of P2p exhibits two distinct peaks at 132.68 and 133.59 eV, corresponding to P-O/PO and P-C/P-N bonds. The B1s spectrum displays three peaks at 190.22, 190.85, and 192.34 eV, corresponding to B-N, B-O, and B-C bonds, respectively. The C1s spectrum shows three peaks at 284.58, 285.67 and 288.26 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O-C, and CO bonds. The N1s spectrum presents three peaks at 399.75, 400.58, and 401.21 eV, corresponding to N-H, N-C, and N-N/N-P bonds. Lastly, the O1s spectrum displays two peaks at 531.56 and 532.99 eV, corresponding to CO and C-O bonds. These comprehensive characterization results confirm the successful synthesis and structural features of Au₅Ir@RFBP-GQD.

To evaluate the catalytic activity, Au, Au@GQD, Au@RFBP-GQD and Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD were synthesized and their cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) curves were measured in a 1.0 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. As shown in Fig. s2, Au@GQD exhibited a more sensitive CV and DPV current response compared to Au, indicating that the introduction of GQD improves the catalytic activity. Since GQD is a semiconductor, its incorporation induces the formation of Schottky heterojunction, which facilitates the redox reactions of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- leading to an increased electrochemical response. Interestingly, replacing GQD with RFBP-GQD resulted in a further increase in CV and DPV currents. This enhancement can be attributed to the unique properties of RFBP-GQD. Unlike GQD, RFBP-GQD contains arginine and folic acid functional groups at the edges of the graphene sheets, along with boron and phosphorus atoms embedded in the carbon lattice. The introduction of these functional groups and heteroatoms reduces the bandgap, thereby accelerating the electron transfer, which leads to increased CV and DPV current. The catalytic activity was further improved by introducing Ir. The combination of Au and Ir significantly enhances the catalytic activity, as demonstrated by the increased CV and DPV currents. Among four synthesized materials, Au₅Ir@RFBP-GQD exhibited the highest CV and DPV currents, indicating superior catalytic activity. This excellent catalytic activity can be attributed to the synergistic effect of Au and Ir, along with the unique semiconductor properties of RFBP-GQD.

Fig. 3A illustrates the schematic for constructing the biosensor for electrochemical detection of atrazine. Initially, hairpin DNA 4 (H4) is immobilized on a gold electrode (GE) via the Au-S bonding. Subsequently, mercaptohexanol (MCH) is introduced onto the surface of H4/GE to block any remaining active sites. The resulting biosensor enables electrochemical detection of atrazine by coupling with a DNA walker. During detection, atrazine specifically hybridizes with AP/AS, releasing free AS. Since AP contains the DNA sequence for the atrazine aptamer, it binds strongly with atrazine to form a stable composite. The released AS then hybridizes with H1, initiating the AS cycle. Through this cycle, one AS can generate numerous catalytic hairpin assemblies (H1/H2/H3, CHA). The CHA binds with three H4 molecules on the MCH/H4/GE, triggering the CHA cycle. This interaction opens the hairpin structure of H4, fully exposing the base sequence that binds with H5, leading to the formation of H4/H5. The opened H4 hybridizes with H5 in H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI, releasing free CHA, which is used to propagate CHA cycle. This process results in the immobilization of H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI redox probes on the biosensor, generating a sensitive electrochemical response via the oxidation and reduction of THI. The CHA cycle enables one CHA molecule to deliver multiple H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI probes to the biosensor, amplifying the detection signal. More importantly, the integration of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD catalysis with the DNA walker ensures highly sensitive and selective detection of atrazine.
walker ensures highly sensitive and selective detection of atrazine.

The CV and EIS of various electrodes were assessed in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- electrolyte to elucidate the biosensor assembly process. Figs. 3B and 3C illustrate that the modification of H4 and MCH results in a reduction in CV current and an increase in charge transfer resistance (Rct), as detailed in Table S1. Since H4 and MCH are non-conductive materials, their modification obstructs certain electron transfer pathways, thereby causing a decrease in CV current. The CV current further decreases after incubation in the tripedal DNA walker solution without H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI due to the formation of CHA/H4, which like H4 and MCH, is nonconductive. However, incubation with H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI significantly increases the CV current and decreases the Rct value. This is because the CHA cycle brings multiple redox probes to the biosensor, and the oxidation and reduction of THI molecules in these redox probes generate a sensitive electrochemical response.

To verify the sensing mechanism, fourteen DNA cycle reaction pathways were analyzed using native PAGE on a Tanon EPS-200 electrophoresis apparatus with a Tanon VE-180B vertical electrophoresis tank (Shanghai Tianneng Technology Co., Ltd, China) (Fig. 3D). One band (AP/AS) appears on the electropherogram of AP+AS (lane 8), verifying AP and AS were converted into AP/AS. One band (AS/H1) appears on electropherogram for AS+H1 (lane 9), verifying that AS combines with H1 to form AP/AS. One band (AS/H1/H2)) appears on electropherogram for AS+H1+H2 (lane 10), confirming the formation of AS/H1/H2. One band (H4/H5) appears on electropherogram for unannealed H4+H5 (lane 11), verifying that H2 hybridizes with H5 to form H4/H5. Three bands (H1/H2/H3, AP and AS) appear on electropherogram for AP+AS+H1+H2+ H3 (lane 12), confirming the triggering of the AS cycle and the formation of CHA (H1/H2/H3). Lane 12 also shows AP and AS bands, indicating that the formation of AP/AS is effectively inhibited in the presence of H1, H2 and H3. Four bright bands (H1/H2/H3, AP, H4/H5, and AS) appear on the electropherogram for AP+AS+H1+H2+H3+H4+H5 (lane 13), confirming that the AS and CHA cycles lead to the production of H4/H5. No AP/AS band is present in lane 13, further indicating that the AP/AS formation is inhibited by the CHA cycle. One AP/AS band and one AS band appear on the electropherogram for AP/AS+H1 (lane 14), as H1 does not directly combine with AP/AS to form new species.

The CV behaviour of the biosensor in 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- electrolyte was also studied by varying scan rate and performing repeated CV measurements. Fig. 3E-G show that the CV current increases rapidly with increasing scan rate. A good linear relationship between the peak current (Ip) and scan rate confirms that the electrode reaction is primarily driven by electrolyte diffusion. Furthermore, minimal variation in CV current after 100 repeated measurements validates the stability of the biosensor and the absence of electrochemically active species leaching from the electrode or other side reactions.

The CV and DPV curves were recorded in both the absence and presence of atrazine to evaluate the electrochemical response of the biosensor. Fig. 4A and B illustrate that the introduction of atrazine significantly increases both CV and DPV currents. This increase occurs because atrazine triggers a cascading DNA cycle, resulting in the immobilization of numerous H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI probes on the biosensor. The oxidation and reduction of THI molecules in these probes generate a sensitive electrochemical response. The DPV technique was chosen for atrazine detection due to its high sensitivity and selectivity.

To optimize the detection conditions, the effects of incubation time and volume of H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI on the DPV peak current were investigated. Fig. 4C shows that the DPV peak current increases rapidly as the incubation time increases, up to 60 min. This is because longer incubation allows more H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI probes to accumulate on the biosensor. However, beyond 60 min, the DPV peak current stabilizes, indicating that the DNA cycle has reached an equilibrium state, and the number of probes on the biosensor remains constant. Similarly, Fig. 4D demonstrates that the DPV peak current increases in tandem with the increase in volume of H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI, up to 10 µL. Volumes below 10 μL do not provide enough H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI probes to sustain the DNA cycle. As the volume increases, more probes are delivered to the biosensor, resulting in a higher DPV peak current. However, the DPV peak current stabilizes when the volume exceeds 10 μL, as this amount is sufficient to saturate the biosensor with probes. Based on these results, an incubation time of 60 min and a probe volume of 10 μL were selected for the electrochemical detection of atrazine to maximize sensitivity.

To evaluate the detection capability of the biosensor, DPV measurements were performed at varying concentrations of atrazine. Fig. 4E illustrates that the DPV current increases sharply with increasing atrazine concentrations. A higher concentration of atrazine molecules releases more free AS sequences, accelerating the DNA cycle and transporting more H5-Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD-THI probes to the biosensor. The oxidation and reduction of these probes generate a more pronounced DPV current, enhancing detection sensitivity. A positive linear relationship was observed between the peak current (Ip) and the logarithmic value of atrazine concentration within the range of 1×10−18 to 1×10−12 M. The regression equation is: Ip (μA) = 41.504 × LOG[Catrazine, M]+ 765.84 with R² of 0.9888. The limit of detection was determined to be 3.4×10−19 M (S/N=3). For comparison, the analytical performance of various methods for electrochemical detection of atrazine is summarized in Table 1. The proposed biosensor exhibits superior sensitivity compared to alternative methods.

Fig. 4G and H and Fig. s3 present the DPV peak currents for 1×10−12 M atrazine across various biosensors, storage durations, and after fifty repeated DPV measurements. The results indicate that the DPV peak current varied by less than 5 % across ten different biosensors, after six weeks of storage, and over fifty repeated measurements. This confirms that the proposed biosensor exhibits excellent reproducibility, storage stability, and repeatability.

The effect of coexisting species and small organic molecules, typically found in natural water on the DPV peak current was also investigated. The results show that 1 mg of Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, C2O4 2-, CO3 2−, PO4 3-, SO4 2−, Cl−, I-, oxalic acid, citric acid, glucose and ascorbic acid caused less than a 3 % change in the DPV peak current produced by1×10−12 M atrazine. These ions and organic molecules were fully removed during the electrode washing steps. Due to the structural similarities, other pesticides from agricultural production may interfere with atrazine detection. However, Fig. 4G demonstrates that only atrazine induces a significant increase in the DPV peak. This is because other pesticides lack complementary base pairs to bind with AP/AS and release free AS. Consequently, no redox probes are introduced to the biosensor, and no DPV peak is observed. These findings confirm that the proposed biosensor has excellent selectivity for detection of atrazine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of atrazine in environmental waters
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental water samples were collected from Wuxi and the atrazine concentrations were determined using the proposed biosensor. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates of atrazine in spiked samples ranged from 96.1 % to 101.7 %, demonstrating the reliability of the biosensor for atrazine detection in environmental water.

This study demonstrates the successful synthesis of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD through the reduction of HAuCl4 and IrCl3 using RFBP-GQD. The synergistic interaction between Au and Ir, along with the formation of a Schottky heterojunction, significantly enhances the catalytic activity. The integration of Au5Ir@RFBP-GQD catalysis with a DNA walker mechanism achieves ultra-high sensitivity and selectivity. The proposed biosensor was effectively applied for the electrochemical detection of atrazine in environmental water samples. Furthermore, this study opens new avenues for the development of noble metal nanoalloys with desirable catalytic properties, offering promising applications in biosensing, catalysis, biomedicine, and energy storage and conversion.

Li Ruiyi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Pan Yue: Investigation. Shen Yirui: Investigation. Wang Xie: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFA0910200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136873.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pan Yue is currently one master candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their application in optical sensors.

Li Ruiyi is currently one associate professor in School of School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Wang Xie is currently one master candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their application in optical sensors

Shen Yirui is currently one associate professor in School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology in China. She received his doctor degree from College of Chemistry, Nanjing University, China, 2019. Her research interest focuses on synthesis and application of advanced materials.

Li Zaijun is one professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2004. He is author of more than 200 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
